data.title: Arxis soars in market debut, jumps 36% after $1.13B IPO
data.description:
Arxis Inc. (ARXS) made a strong entrance into public markets Thursday, with shares climbing 36% in their first day of trading after the company raised $1.13 billion in an expanded initial public offering.

The Bloomfield, Connecticut-based manufacturer, backed by Arcline Investment Management, began trading at $38 per share on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, well above its IPO price of $28.

Based on its outstanding shares, the debut values the aerospace and defense components supplier at roughly $15.4 billion.

Arxis increased the size of its offering to 40.5 million shares, up from the 37.7 million originally planned, pricing at the top end of its $25 to $28 target range—an indication of strong investor demand.

The company’s listing comes as rising global defense spending continues to attract investor attention. In the U.S., expectations that policymakers will prioritize expanding and modernizing missile systems have added momentum to companies tied to military supply chains.

Defense and space applications represent Arxis’ (ARXS) largest business segment, accounting for about 47% of projected 2025 revenue, according to regulatory filings. Commercial aerospace makes up another 23%, underscoring the company’s exposure to both military and civil aviation markets.

Arxis joins (ARXS) a growing pipeline of industrial companies tapping equity markets. Aevex Corp., backed by Madison Dearborn Partners, is pursuing a listing that could raise up to $336 million, while nuclear developer X-Energy Inc. is targeting as much as $814 million. Meanwhile, Madison Air Solutions (MAIR) recently completed a $2.23 billion offering, marking one of the largest U.S. industrial IPOs in decades.

data.title: Seagate lands on J.P. Morgan's 'Positive Catalyst Watch' due to strength of HDD market
data.description:
Seagate Technology (STX) was placed on J.P. Morgan's "Positive Catalyst Watch" list as it is well positioned in the HDD industry to benefit from strong demand related to data center demand and pricing tailwinds.

The financial firm also increased its price target on the stock to $600 from $525.

"STX is ahead in terms of technology transition and is already shipping next-generation HAMR-based solutions to data center customers," said J.P. Morgan analysts, led by Samik Chatterjee, in a Thursday investor report. "Near- and medium-term fundamentals continue to improve as the industry is being disciplined in adding new capacity and focusing on profitable growth."

"Downside risks to our rating and price target include a slowdown in cloud capex spending on AI infrastructure, which could present risk to demand growth forecasts for the HDD industry despite the current robust pipeline of investment announcements," he added. 

Seagate is scheduled to release its third quarter fiscal 2026 earnings report post-market on April 28. A consensus estimate expects adjusted EPS of $3.48 and GAAP EPS of $3.19 on revenue of $2.95B. During the third quarter of fiscal 2025, Seagate reported adjusted EPS of $1.90 and revenue of $2.16B.

data.title: U.S.-Iran peace deal could take 6 months to finalize – report
data.description:
Some Gulf Arab and European leaders believe a U.S.-Iran peace deal will take approximately six months to finalize and are urging both sides to extend their ceasefire to cover that negotiating window, according to officials from the regions, Bloomberg reported.

The leaders are demanding the immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to restore energy flows, warning privately that a global food crisis may develop if the vital waterway isn’t opened by next month.

Brent crude (CO1:COM) has climbed about 3.5% to more than $98 a barrel, with oil prices up 35% since the conflict began in late February, and Gulf economies have been unable to export normal levels of oil, liquefied natural gas, aluminum, and fertilizers.

Gulf states believe Iran is still pursuing a nuclear weapon despite the U.S.-Israeli bombardment and want any peace deal to ban Iran from enriching uranium or possessing long-range ballistic missiles.

The UAE has emphasized the need for “a comprehensive and sustained approach that addresses Iran’s full range of threats, including its nuclear capabilities, ballistic missiles, drones, military capabilities, and affiliated proxies and terrorist groups.”

Washington and Tehran are currently weighing a two-week extension of their truce, which ends Tuesday, though contentious issues remain, including Iran’s demand for indefinite control over the Hormuz Strait, sanctions relief, and the ongoing war in Lebanon between Israel and Tehran-backed Hezbollah.

President Donald Trump announced a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon on Thursday, which some officials said could help advance the broader Iran negotiations.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on the U.S.-Iran war

  • Israel, Lebanon agree on 10-day ceasefire
  • U.S. naval blockade of Iran turns away 13 ships, ramps up pressure on Tehran - reports
  • Polymarket odds for Warsh Fed confirmation by May 15 hit new low
  • U.S., Iran could resume peace talks in Pakistan next week - report
  • White House budget director won't estimate cost of Iran war

data.title: U.S. Army moves to enhance Gray Eagle drone with new intelligence features
data.description:
The U.S. Army is upgrading its MQ-1C Gray Eagle Extended Range drone with new electronic intelligence, or ELINT, capabilities under a contract awarded to General Atomics Aeronautical Systems, the company said Wednesday.

 

 

The upgrade is designed to improve the aircraft’s ability to detect and analyze enemy air defense systems from long distances, allowing commanders to gather intelligence while keeping the drone outside high-risk areas. The capability is also expected to support coordination between unmanned systems and crewed aircraft, a growing focus for the Army as it looks to reduce risk to pilots.

According to the company, the ELINT system will enable the drone to identify and locate radar and other electronic signals associated with integrated air defense systems. General Atomics is working with the Army’s intelligence and spectrum warfare office and contractor SNC to incorporate radar detection and signal collection technology into the platform.

The Gray Eagle ER has been used in recent Army demonstrations with a range of intelligence and networking tools, including communications intelligence, radar imaging and mobile network radios. These systems are part of a broader effort to make the aircraft adaptable to different missions through a modular design that allows new sensors and equipment to be added more easily.

The drone was also deployed during the Army’s Project Convergence Capstone exercise last year, where it carried out surveillance and targeting tasks in a contested electronic environment. During the exercise, it also served as a communications relay, linking units operating in terrain where direct connections were limited.

Army officials have emphasized the importance of systems that can operate in environments where adversaries may attempt to jam or disrupt communications. The Gray Eagle’s upgraded capabilities are intended to help maintain situational awareness and improve coordination across units in those conditions.

The contract reflects the Army’s continued investment in unmanned systems that can perform intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance missions while integrating with broader battlefield networks.

data.title: NextNRG outlines $750M smart microgrid pipeline as 2025 revenue reaches $81.8M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NextNRG (NXXT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman & CEO Michael Farkas framed 2025’s scale-up by saying, "In 2024, NextNRG generated $27.8 million in revenue. While in 2025, we generated $81.8 million... That is about 195% growth in 1 single year," and added that "Our on-site mobile fueling business was the driver of this growth."
  • Farkas tied expansion to integration activity, saying, "Following the completed merger of NextNRG and EzFill, we integrated 2 acquisitions, Shell assets and Yoshi Mobility," and that the company entered "4 new major markets: Phoenix, Austin, San Antonio and Houston."
  • On operating momentum and unit economics, Farkas said, "We posted 7 consecutive months of record revenue," and highlighted fueling margins: "Our full year gross margin in fueling was 8.4%. By Q4, it climbed to 10.4%."
  • On customer concentration and competitive displacement, Farkas stated, "Our largest commercial fleet customer, the largest global online retailer is actively cutting other fuel vendors in certain markets. And replacing them with us, NextNRG."
  • On the energy infrastructure segment, Farkas said, "We closed our first power purchase agreements, Sunnyside and Topanga Terrace Rehabilitation & Subacute Care Centers, both in California," describing them as "long-term structured agreements with annual escalators built in" and adding, "Our pipeline of planned smart microgrid projects stands at approximately $750 million."
  • CFO Joel Kleiner emphasized the gap between GAAP loss and cash costs: "Our GAAP net loss for 2025 was $88.2 million," while "The largest driver is stock-based compensation which is totaled at $42.6 million. This is entirely noncash."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit 2026 revenue or EPS guidance in the prepared remarks or the submitted Q&A.
  • Kleiner described a 2026 funding and balance-sheet focus: "The goal for 2026 is to reduce our dependence on high-cost short-term debt by growing operating cash flow, increasing working capital and closing contracts that carry their own financing."
  • Farkas described the intended financial impact of energy contracts versus fueling: "We expect the margin profile on a stabilized microgrid to be significantly higher than what we generate in fueling," and said the company believes it "has the potential to meaningfully change the financial profile of this company."

Financial Results

  • The company reported full-year 2025 revenue of $81.8 million (vs. $27.8 million in 2024), cost of sales of $74.9 million (vs. $26 million), and gross profit of $6.9 million (vs. $1.8 million).
  • The company reported full-year 2025 GAAP net loss of $88.2 million, including stock-based compensation of $42.6 million, interest expense of $17.3 million (including $9.6 million in noncash amortization of debt discount), and an $8.5 million impairment charge.
  • The company reported adjusted EBITDA loss of $17.1 million for 2025 (vs. $8.9 million in 2024) and net cash used in operating activities of $16.7 million.
  • The company reported Q4 revenue of approximately $23 million, with monthly revenue of $7.4 million in October, $7.5 million in November, and $8 million in December.

Q&A

  • Sharon Cohen (submitted question): "You recorded $42.6 million in stock-based compensation in 2025. Who received that compensation?" Joel Kleiner: "A lot of that work was compensated in equity... It's not something you should expect to see at this level going forward."
  • Sharon Cohen (submitted question): "Cash at year-end was $384,000 and the working capital deficit since approximately $25 million... How does NextNRG get through this next year 2026?" CFO Kleiner: "We are not managing this business on $384,000. We're managing it on a combination of operational cash flow, debt facilities and the capital markets access we've consistently demonstrated."
  • Sharon Cohen (submitted question): "When those contracts do start generating revenue, what does the margin profile actually look like?" CEO Farkas: "It is a completely different margin profile... We expect the margin profile on a stabilized microgrid to be significantly higher than what we generate in fueling."
  • Sharon Cohen (submitted question): "Given the current cash position and working capital deficit, what does the path to cash flow breakeven look like?" CEO Farkas: "There are 3 things... [fueling]... close and monetize energy infrastructure contracts... and... rightsize our operating expenses relative to where the business actually is today."
  • Sharon Cohen (submitted question): "How is management thinking about capital allocation?" CEO Farkas: "The fueling business funds itself at this point. It generates positive operating cash flow," and on microgrids, "The capital to build each project comes with the project through project financing, not from corporate balance sheet."

Sentiment Analysis

  • The submitted questions carried a slightly negative tone centered on dilution and liquidity, including, "How does NextNRG get through this next year 2026?" and the working capital deficit reference.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (growth, contracts, pipeline) but more defensive/clarifying on liquidity, including Kleiner’s, "the cash position... does not tell the whole story" and "We are not managing this business on $384,000."
  • Versus Q3, the current quarter’s discussion placed more direct emphasis on year-end cash, working capital deficit, and reliance on financing, while continuing to stress scaling and margin improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management emphasized full-year scale and the transition narrative: "The fuel business funds the operation today, the energy business is where the exponential growth will come from."
  • In Q3, management highlighted expansion actions and margin drivers (route density and supplier discounts) and disclosed signing two PPAs with an expected 28-year revenue period; in Q4, management again referenced the first PPAs and described a longer-duration framing, saying some agreements are "as many as 3 decades" and added a pipeline figure of "approximately $750 million."
  • Q3 included commentary on refinancing and converting debt to equity; Q4 shifted to explaining GAAP loss components and explicitly addressing dilution and liquidity mechanics through submitted questions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Liquidity and leverage were directly raised, including reported year-end cash of $384,000 and a working capital deficit of approximately $25 million, alongside discussion of reliance on "high interest instruments to fund operations."
  • Kleiner’s mitigation framing centered on access and structure: "We have active debt facilities in place," "continue to have access to capital markets," and described project-level financing where contracts "bring project level financing structures that are standard in the industry."
  • Execution timing risk for microgrids was described by Farkas: projects require "engineering studies, permitting, utility interconnection approvals, project financing" and can involve decision-making that "can span years."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that 2025 revenue grew to $81.8 million while gross profit reached $6.9 million, alongside a Q4 revenue run-rate of approximately $23 million and improving fueling gross margins. In Q&A, management addressed dilution tied to $42.6 million of stock-based compensation and described a 2026 plan to reduce reliance on high-cost short-term debt using operating cash flow, capital markets access, and project-level financing as energy contracts move toward construction. Management also highlighted the first signed PPAs and described an energy infrastructure pipeline of approximately $750 million as the primary long-term growth focus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Qualcomm downgraded to Neutral and placed on 'negative catalyst watch': JPMorgan
data.description:
Qualcomm (QCOM) was downgraded to Neutral from Overweight by J.P. Morgan due to a combination of rising competition in the data center space and downside risks to estimates in the near term.

The financial firm also lowered its price target to $140 from $185 and placed the stock on its "Negative Catalyst Watch" list.

"Qualcomm's long-term diversification strategy beyond smartphones has been part of our Overweight thesis on the shares through periods of macro and company-specific volatility in revenue drivers," said J.P. Morgan analysts, led by Samik Chatterjee, in an investor report. 

"In relation to the datacenter CPU and NPU opportunity within Datacenters, succesful execution could drive an inflection in non-smartphone revenues and deliver diversification ahead of its planned roadmap; but while the company is still working on its roadmap with customers in building pipeline of opportunities across both datacenter CPUs and NPUs, recent announcements from Arm (ARM) and Nvidia (NVDA) focused on Datacenter CPUs as well as chipsets to address inference use cases are increasing the competitive intensity and driving us to look more for execution datapoints from Qualcomm, which we expect will take time to emerge."

In the smartphone market, J.P. Morgan expects Qualcomm to face near-term pressure due to the memory supply shortage combined with its share loss from two of its premier customers, Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (SSNLF). 

In addition, the financial firm also pointed out macroeconomic concerns related to Qualcomm's Internet of Things and Automotive business segments.

J.P. Morgan reduced its revenue estimates for Qualcomm Technology Licensing. It reduced its fiscal year 2026 QTL revenue forecast to $5.3B from $5.5B. It also expects Qualcomm's wireless chipset technology, or QCT, business to decline by 22% in calendar year 2026 compared to the 17% consensus estimate. 

Qualcomm is slated to report its second quarter fiscal 2026 financial results post-market on April 29. A consensus estimate expects adjusted EPS of $2.56 and GAAP EPS of $1.88 on $10.57B in revenue.

During the second quarter of fiscal 2025, Qualcomm reported adjusted EPS of $2.85 and revenue of $10.84B.

data.title: Insteel outlines $20M 2026 capex as it expects gradual gross margin improvement in Q3
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insteel Industries (IIIN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Despite falling well short of our expected financial performance in Q2, we believe the upturn in business activity we reported previously is still intact." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • "We regret that we experienced both of these phenomena during Q2, but we're confident that short-term weather conditions and project delays neither create nor destroy demand and that postponed demand will be evident during the balance of fiscal 2026." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • "Our second quarter results were weaker than expected, reflecting the combined impact of winter weather disruptions, lower spreads and higher unit conversion costs." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "As we enter the third quarter, we expect several factors to support a recovery in gross margin." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "While we continue to believe that demand will be solid during 2026, we will reduce costs if this forecast fails to materialize." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Outlook

  • "Although we are still early in the third quarter, recent order activity has been solid, with April shipments trending above forecasted loans." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We expect these recent pricing actions, along with the additional price increase implemented in April to provide further benefit in the coming periods as they are more fully reflected in our realized pricing." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "At this point, however, we do not expect to be in a cost reduction mode driven by demand-related concerns." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • "As we closed out the second quarter, we remain encouraged by the demand trends we're seeing across our core end markets... At the same time, we recognize that these external factors could influence the pace of the activity in the near term." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Financial Results

  • "Net earnings for the quarter were $5.2 million or $0.27 per share compared with $10.2 million or $0.52 per diluted share in the same period last year." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "Shipments for the quarter declined 5.9% from the prior year but increased 6.9% sequentially from the first quarter." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "Gross profit declined $8 million year-over-year, $16.5 million, and gross margin narrowed to 9.6%." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "Operating cash flow provided $4.8 million in the current quarter compared with using $3.3 million of cash in the prior year period." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We incurred $4.4 million in capital expenditures in the quarter... and we remain committed to our full year target of $20 million." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We ended the quarter with $15.1 million of cash on hand and no borrowings outstanding on our $100 million revolving credit facility, providing us ample liquidity and financial flexibility going forward." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Q&A

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked how much delayed projects weighed on shipments and what drove delays; President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz answered, "this is a delay of business. It's not a cancellation" and added, "we should begin shipping it in the current quarter... this project will go through our fiscal year and into 2027." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked whether April shipment strength reflected delayed projects or weather catch-up; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "I don't think any of it is related to project delays" and added, "the current performance... is pretty solid relative to our expectations and our prices are coming up as we expected them to." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about freight cost pass-through and recovery; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we debated surcharges or we debated price increases, and we've elected just to increase our prices" and added, "I wouldn't say we recovered them prospectively." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked magnitude and cadence of price increases; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we've implemented 3 since the first of the year" and described a lag effect: "we are honoring the commitments that we've made" and "it would be the next... orders that are affected by price increases." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about tariff clarification and potential refunds; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said Section 232 clarifications "don't have a whole lot of impact on us" and added, "we certainly will not be booking any kinds of receivables for tariff collections because I think it's highly improbable that it will happen in any simplistic kind of way." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked whether data center builds are prone to being pushed out; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we don't think that the data center phenomenon goes away in 2026 or '27" and added, "I think you have 5 solid years of data center activity." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone appeared neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on shipment deferrals, freight recovery, tariff mechanics, and the risk of data center timing being "pushed to the right." (Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates)

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly negative on Q2 performance but more confident about recovery, including "we're confident" on postponed demand and "we expect" gross margin recovery drivers; in Q&A, management was more matter-of-fact and occasionally cautionary, including "I wouldn't say we recovered them prospectively" and "we certainly will not be booking any kinds of receivables." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz; VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s more upbeat framing ("we delivered a strong start to the year" and "we are encouraged"), the current quarter emphasized disruption and lag effects while keeping a recovery narrative, including "we expect several factors to support a recovery in gross margin." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized momentum and acquisitions, including "The upturn in business activity... continued" and "our fiscal 2025 acquisitions continue to perform well," while Q2 emphasized underperformance and temporary disruption: "we were affected during Q2 by weather-related and non-weather-related circumstances." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • Q1 described pricing as stable sequentially with "we did not take additional pricing actions during the current period" and then a January increase; Q2 emphasized repeated actions and lag, including "we've implemented 3 since the first of the year" and an April increase expected to flow through realized pricing. (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz; VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • Both quarters highlighted wire rod tightness and offshore sourcing, but Q2 updated working capital pressure: "Net working capital rose approximately $45 million over the last 12 months" and reiterated continued importing "until such time as domestic availability improves." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Winter weather is a fact of life in our business" and Q2 experienced "severe and prolonged" conditions that disrupted construction activity and operating schedules. (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • "The broader macroeconomic backdrop continues to evolve, including the risk of renewed inflation, uncertainty around the timing of interest rate cuts, potential changes in tariff policy and the geopolitical developments affecting energy and shipping costs." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "The economics of offshore transactions... require the purchase of large quantities with the resulting impact on inventories and net working capital requirements" as the company imports wire rod amid reduced domestic capacity; management said it has "some options to mitigate this adverse impact." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a weather- and timing-impacted quarter with weaker spreads and higher conversion costs, while maintaining that demand was delayed rather than lost. Entering Q3, management pointed to improving seasonal demand, traction from multiple price increases (including one in April), more favorable raw material carrying values, and higher operating rates as the basis for a gradual margin recovery, while highlighting ongoing external risks spanning tariffs, freight, and broader macro uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta raises price of Quest VR headsets to compensate for higher chip costs
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) is raising the cost of its Quest virtual reality headsets to compensate for the increased cost of memory chips.

“We’re making this change because the cost of building high-performance VR hardware has risen significantly,” the company said on its website. “The global surge in the price of critical components—specifically memory chips—is impacting almost every category of consumer electronics, including VR.”

Beginning April 19, the price of the Meta Quest 3S (128 GB) and Meta Quest 3S (256 GB) will go up $50 to $349.99 and $449.99, respectively, and the cost of the Meta Quest 3 (256 GB) will increase by $100 to $599.99.   

Updated pricing will also apply to refurbished units. 

While Meta (META) reassured its customers that it remains committed to investing in VR and “leading the category,” the company recently cut 10% of its Reality Labs staff amid a pivot from virtual reality to generative artificial intelligence.

data.title: Musk's xAI to rent computing power to Cursor: report
data.description:
Amid the insatiable demand for computing power, xAI (X.AI) appears likely to rent some of its capacity to artificial intelligence startup Cursor, Business Insider reported. 

Cursor is likely to train its latest AI model, known as Composer 2.5, on “thousands” of GPUs that xAI has obtained from suppliers, such as Nvidia (NVDA), the news outlet reported. The move could position xAI, which is now a part of SpaceX (SPACE), as a potential alternative to the hyperscalers, such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), and the neo-clouds, such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS), the news outlet added. It could also boost xAI's revenue streams as SpaceX reportedly prepares to go public amid high investor expectations.

Elon Musk, who co-founded xAI a few years ago, apologized in March for not building xAI “right” and said it was being rebuilt.

Cursor, which reportedly hit $2B in annual recurring revenue last month, is in talks to raise additional funding that would value the AI coding company at $50B.

Neither xAI nor Cursor immediately responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Sustained oil prices essential before markets can see fundamental risk-taking – analyst
data.description:
Sustained lower oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) are essential before the market can see a return of fundamental risk-taking, according to Alli McCartney, managing director at UBS Alignment Partners.

While recent rallies have pushed markets near all-time highs, McCartney cautioned that the current momentum reflects technical factors rather than renewed investor confidence.

In an interview with CNBC, McCartney explained that the recent recovery in beaten-down sectors like software (XSW), (IGV) and alternative asset managers stems from short covering rather than fresh investment.

“What you are seeing in terms of the technicals of the market or what’s actually happening at a micro level is not exactly renewed risk-taking yet, but rather short covering,” she said.

Despite the technical nature of the rally, McCartney acknowledged bullish tailwinds from an impressive earnings season showing 17% growth. She noted the breadth is particularly encouraging, with average companies up eight to 10% rather than gains concentrated in just a few top performers.

“That is a very different level of breadth than we’ve seen in past earning seasons,” she observed.

However, oil remains the dominant bearish concern. “The biggest bearish story out there is still the price of oil and the long-term macro effects, not only in the U.S., but especially outside of the U.S.,” McCartney said.

She added that while markets may have overreacted to the downside, the rapid swing back upward could also prove excessive.

On the positive side, McCartney pointed to ISM manufacturing entering expansionary territory for the first time in five years and a labor market that is “precarious but potentially balanced.”

Still, she emphasized that April and May data will be critical in revealing the true impact of elevated fuel costs on consumers.

The real-world burden of high gas prices (XB1:COM) remains a persistent concern. McCartney recounted paying $8 a gallon during a recent trip to California, underscoring the strain on American consumers. “That doesn’t go away,” she said.
